query: Anti-centromere antibodies association with different autoimmune conditions

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Sclerosis (Scleroderma) -- Evaluation -- Autoantibody Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Anti-centromere antibody: Anti-centromere antibodies target 4 centromere antigens (CENP -B, -A, -C, D) and are predominantly observed in limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis, although they may occasionally occur in diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis. They can also manifest in conditions such as Sjögren syndrome and systemic lupus erythematosus. Anti-centromere antibodies are associated with an increased risk of PAH. Furthermore, they are linked to limited cutaneous involvement, a reduced likelihood of interstitial lung disease, and generally better survival rates compared to other autoantibodies.

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Several patterns of ANAs have been reported, including speckled, homogenous, centromere, cytoplasmic, nucleolar, and dense fine speckled patterns. With the availability of more specific ANAs targeting specific antigens, the staining patterns of ANAs are not considered significant enough by themselves. ANAs with a dense, fine speckled pattern (anti-DFS70) are considered least pathological, and patients with ANAs with this pattern rarely develop systemic autoimmune diseases. The speckled pattern is seen when ANAs are directed against the antigens such as SSA, SSB, Smith, ribonucleoprotein. The homogenous pattern is associated with ANAs targeted at histones, chromatin, and dsDNA, while the centromere pattern is associated with Anti-centromere antibodies seen in limited systemic sclerosis. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Antinuclear Antibodies (ANA) -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: While IIF-ANA remains the primary initial testing method, other methods, including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) and immunoassays, offer confirmatory testing for specific ANAs. ELISA offers quantitative screening for specific ANAs and has proven to perform comparably to IIF, and commercial panels for antibodies to SSA/Ro, SSB/La, Sm, Scl-70, Jo-1, and centromeres are available. [17] In addition, multiplex immunoassays offer similar benefits and use a series of known antigen-coated beads, and when combined with patient serum, indicate their specific antibody targets. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Sjogren Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: One group consists of younger individuals with low systemic disease activity but a high symptom burden, typically including fatigue, dryness, and pain. These patients often exhibit increased B-cell activation, eg, hypergammaglobulinemia, reduced C4 levels, and higher seropositivity for SSA or SSB. [30] A second group presents with both high systemic disease activity and a high symptom burden. These individuals frequently possess additional autoantibodies, including anti-RNP or anti-centromere antibodies.

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Anti-Smith antibodies are seen in less than 30% of SLE patients but have 99% specificity for SLE. They are observed more in African-American patients with SLE. Anti-Smith antibodies in SLE are usually always associated with Anti-U1-RNP antibodies, which are present in up to 30% of SLE patients. Anti-U1-RNP antibodies can also be seen in mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD), although in MCTD, a disorder that is closely related to SLE. Anti-ribosomal-P antibodies are very specific for SLE, although their prevalence in SLE is less than 5%, and they may correlate with neuropsychiatric manifestations of SLE. Anti-histone antibodies are not specific for drug-induced lupus and can be seen in 50% to 70% of cases of SLE. Anti-centromere and anti-topoisomerase-I (SCL70) antibodies are seen in systemic sclerosis and rarely in SLE (less than 5%). Anti-histidyl-tRNA-synthetase antibodies are seen in myositis. Patients with SLE may also have antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulants, anti-cardiolipin, and anti-beta-2-glycoprotein I antibodies) and are associated with more thrombotic events and adverse pregnancy-related outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Mixed Connective Tissue Disease -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Immunological markers include high titer speckled pattern anti-nuclear antibody (typically >1280) and high titer anti-U1-RNP. The sensitivity of the U1-RNP antibody is 100%, but its specificity is lower. Another potential marker for MCTD is the presence of survival of motor neuron (SMN) antibody directed to SMN and its complex. In a study comparing the prevalence of anti-SMN complex antibodies between patients with systemic lupus erythematosus and MCTD, the antibodies were present in 40% of MCTD patients, whereas only 10% of patients with systemic lupus erythematosus had the antibody. In this study, the estimated specificity of the anti-SMN antibody was 91.2% for MCTD. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Antinuclear Antibodies (ANA) -- Clinical Significance -- Scleroderma
retrieved_docs.content: Scleroderma, or systemic sclerosis, involves progressive fibrosis of the skin and organs. Scleroderma presents either as a limited form or a diffuse cutaneous form. The diagnostic basis is a combination of clinical symptoms and increased ANA titers. Scl-70 is highly correlated with scleroderma, while anti-centromere antibodies are moderately correlated. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Antinuclear Antibodies (ANA) -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: IIF patterns correlate to specific ANA subtypes, and pattern recognition is a valuable tool in ANA testing. Homogenous fluorescence pattern typically suggests antibodies directed at dsDNA, histones, or nucleosomes. [1] A membranous pattern may show antibodies to membrane proteins. Antibodies directed to other nuclear antigens correlate with speckled fluorescent patterns. Anti-Smith antibodies fluoresce in a course-speckled pattern. Anti-SSA/Ro and anti-SSB/La form a fine-speckled pattern. Discrete speckles represent antibodies targeted to the centromeres in cells undergoing interphase. Nucleolar speckles are associated with antibodies directed at DNA topoisomerase (Scl-70). The speckled cytoplasmic pattern suggests antibodies to aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase (Jo-1). [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Sclerosis (Scleroderma) -- Prognosis -- Prognostic Factors and Associations in Systemic Sclerosis
retrieved_docs.content: African-American race: Early disease onset, severe disease, interstitial lung disease, and scleroderma renal crisis. Late age of diagnosis: PAH and poor outcomes after scleroderma renal crisis. Diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis: Diffuse skin disease, large joint inflammatory arthritis, tendon friction rubs, joint contractures, distal bone osteolysis, scleroderma renal crisis, interstitial lung disease, and pericardial effusion. Limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis: Limited skin disease, calcinosis, PAH, and hypothyroidism. Multiple telangiectasias: PAH. Pericardial effusion: Scleroderma renal crisis. Tendon friction rubs: Scleroderma renal crisis and a poor overall prognosis. New-onset anemia: Scleroderma renal crisis. Corticosteroid use: Scleroderma renal crisis. Anti-centromere antibody: Limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis and PAH. Lower risk of interstitial lung disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Antinuclear Antibodies (ANA) -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic autoimmune disorders affect 3-5% of the general population, and ANAs are one of the few specific disease markers in their diagnosis. [16] Therefore, ANA testing is often the first step in diagnosing systemic autoimmune connective tissue disorders. Laboratories report the staining pattern and the titer of ANA as an indication for further testing. The presence of ANAs and their subtypes increase the likelihood of a systemic autoimmune disorder, and there are notable clinical correlations between the ANA subtypes and autoimmune connective tissue disorders. However, they do not necessarily confirm that an individual has or will develop an autoimmune disease. Although positive ANA results help diagnose several autoimmune disorders, the negative ANA titer is also expected in some specific inflammatory conditions, for instance, ankylosing spondylitis. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Autoimmunity -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) is used to detect specific antibodies. Many autoimmune diseases have strong associations with the presence of certain antibodies. Rheumatoid factor, an autoantibody that attacks the Fc region of the IgG immunoglobulin, is 1 such molecule found in many autoimmune conditions. It is associated with rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), Sjogren syndrome, cryoglobulinemia, and interstitial fibrosis. A more specific autoantibody for rheumatoid arthritis that can be tested for is anticyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP). Several more examples of key autoantibodies and associated diseases are listed below. While this list is not exhaustive, it reflects very common, high-yield autoantibody associations. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Mixed Connective Tissue Disease -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Due to nonspecific symptoms and different organ involvement, MCTD mimics several other conditions, especially other connective tissue diseases. Differential diagnoses are as follows: Systemic lupus erythematosus is an autoimmune disease characterized by the presence of an anti-nuclear antibody and varied manifestations, which may include inflammatory arthritis, mucocutaneous disease, serositis, pleuritis, pericarditis, renal disease, fevers, neuropsychiatric symptoms, leukopenia, autoimmune hemolysis, thrombocytopenia, antiphospholipid antibodies, low complements, and the presence of an anti-double stranded DNA antibody or anti-Smith antibody.

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency -- Evaluation -- Autoimmune Studies [15] [16]
retrieved_docs.content: Anti-nuclear antibodies (ANA) Detection of specific auto-immune antibodies for systemic disorders (anti-ds DNA, rheumatoid factor, anti-histones, anti-Smith, anti-(SS-A) and anti-(SS-B) Detection of anti-RBC, antiplatelet, and anti-neutrophil Testing for organ-specific auto-immune antibodies

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Sclerosis (Scleroderma) -- Evaluation -- Autoantibody Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Autoantibodies are an essential diagnostic tool, offering predictive insights into disease phenotype and prognosis. Antinuclear antibodies, detected through direct immunofluorescence, are positive in over 90% of systemic sclerosis cases. [38] In instances where antinuclear antibodies are negative, it is imperative to rule out other potential diagnoses before confirming systemic sclerosis. Specific autoantibodies, with a positivity rate of 60% to 70%, offer further diagnostic specificity. These antibodies, as mentioned below, which are mutually exclusive, can manifest several months to years before the clinical onset of systemic sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 52
retrieved_docs.content: range of autoimmune hematologic and rheumatologic disorders are interconnected within the broader context of circulatory and connective‑tissue diseases, informing comprehensive patient evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1239
retrieved_docs.content: The collection “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” comprises a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions that share an autoimmune basis. Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is considered an autoimmune disorder, as are autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية) and cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد). Arthritis (التهاب المفاصل) and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are also members of this autoimmune group, illustrating the systemic nature of immune‑mediated disease. Although non‑immune hemolytic anemia (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية) is listed among these entities, it does not result from autoantibody‑mediated red‑cell destruction; its inclusion reflects clinical overlap and underscores the importance of distinguishing immune from non‑immune mechanisms when evaluating hemolysis. Recognizing that these diverse conditions are linked by an underlying autoimmune process helps clinicians diagnose, monitor, and manage patients with immune‑driven pathology more effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1332
retrieved_docs.content: Arthritis, acquired hemolytic anemia, autoimmune‑mediated hemolytic anemia, non‑immune hemolytic anemia, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions (SMQ). This grouping highlights that arthritis can arise from autoimmune mechanisms, while hemolytic anemias may be either driven by autoantibodies (autoimmune hemolytic anemia) or occur through other, non‑immune pathways (non‑immune hemolytic anemia). Coronary artery inflammation is likewise recognized as an autoimmune manifestation affecting the vascular system. Understanding that these diverse diseases share a common autoimmune framework aids clinicians in recognizing overlapping pathogenic processes and in applying immunomodulatory strategies where appropriate.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2276
retrieved_docs.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية) encompasses several distinct conditions, including التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). Each of these diseases is characterized by an inappropriate immune response that targets the body’s own tissues, leading to joint inflammation, destruction of red blood cells, or inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities share a common autoimmune basis aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and applying immunomodulatory therapies across this spectrum of immune‑mediated illnesses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1539
retrieved_docs.content: Wiskott‑Aldrich anemia, aplastic anemia, arthritis, and cold‑type hemolytic anemia are all classified as members of the immune disorders/autoimmune (SMQ) category. By grouping these diverse conditions together, the classification highlights that each involves dysregulation of the immune system, whether through auto‑reactive mechanisms, immune‑mediated destruction of blood cells, or inflammatory joint disease. Recognizing these diseases as part of the broader autoimmune spectrum aids clinicians in understanding their shared pathophysiology, guiding diagnostic evaluation and informing therapeutic strategies that target immune pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 139
retrieved_docs.content: how specific autoimmune and hematologic diseases fit within broader disease families, identify pharmacologic options and contraindications for their management, connect advanced nuclear‑medicine diagnostic modalities, and clarify the hierarchical organization of symptoms and immune‑related disorders for comprehensive patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), arthritis (التهاب المفاصل), acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب), autoimmune‑mediated hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية), and coronary arteritis (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are all classified as members of the autoimmune disorder group (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية SMQ). These conditions share a common pathophysiology in which the immune system mistakenly attacks self‑tissues, leading to hematologic abnormalities in the various anemias and inflammatory damage in joints and coronary arteries. Recognizing this shared autoimmune basis guides clinicians toward similar diagnostic approaches—such as autoantibody testing—and therapeutic strategies, including immunosuppressive or immunomodulatory treatments, while emphasizing the need to monitor for systemic involvement across both blood‑forming and vascular systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 713
retrieved_docs.content: The category “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions, including فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (aplastic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (hemolytic anemia not caused by autoimmunity), التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). These entities are grouped together because they involve immune system dysregulation, whether through autoantibody‑mediated attacks, cell‑mediated immune injury, or broader immune‑related inflammatory processes. While most of the listed disorders are driven by autoimmune mechanisms, the non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia is included in the classification for organizational purposes despite its different pathogenesis. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying immune component helps clinicians identify common diagnostic pathways and consider immunomodulatory therapies across different organ systems, thereby improving patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is closely linked to داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي, an anti‑basement membrane disease of the kidney, and it also forms a key component of متلازمة رئية كلوية, a pulmonary‑renal syndrome that involves simultaneous injury to the lungs and kidneys. This interrelationship highlights the importance of recognizing necrotizing glomerulonephritis not only as a severe renal pathology but also as a manifestation of systemic autoimmune processes that can affect multiple organ systems, guiding comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and coordinated therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1148
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus (الذئبة الحمراء المجموعية) is clinically associated with other circulatory disease, and this category of circulatory disease is classified as a subtype of connective tissue disorder (اضطراب الأنسجة الضامة). This relationship underscores that cardiovascular manifestations observed in lupus are part of the broader spectrum of connective tissue diseases, highlighting the importance of integrated cardiovascular evaluation and management in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 263
retrieved_docs.content: for SLE and connective‑tissue disorders include trioxsalen and zlato. Overall, the knowledge base integrates hematologic abnormalities (anemia, leukopenia, immunoglobulin deficiency), autoimmune connective‑tissue disease, related weakness and fatigue, ill‑defined symptom categories, and both therapeutic and contraindicated drugs, while highlighting the importance of recognizing transient cerebrovascular events and associated clinical terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1482
retrieved_docs.content: The classification “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses several distinct conditions that share an autoimmune basis. Within this group, التهاب المفاصل is recognized as an autoimmune form of arthritis, فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد represents cold‑agglutinin autoimmune hemolytic anemia, and التهاب الشريان التاجي denotes inflammation of the coronary arteries driven by autoimmune mechanisms. Although فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية is listed among these entities, it is in fact a hemolytic anemia that does not arise from autoimmunity and therefore should be distinguished from the true autoimmune members of the “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” category. Recognizing which disorders are truly autoimmune is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic targeting, and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2327
retrieved_docs.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)) includes a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions. Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is classified as an autoimmune disease, as is non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) and acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب). Inflammatory joint disease (التهاب المفاصل) and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are also considered members of this autoimmune group. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying autoimmune mechanism guides clinicians toward appropriate immunomodulatory treatments and vigilant monitoring for systemic involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1385
retrieved_docs.content: The SMQ category of immune disorders/autoimmune comprises a diverse group of hematologic and rheumatologic conditions. Within this category, aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is recognized as an autoimmune-mediated failure of bone‑marrow production. Non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) is also classified as an autoimmune disorder in which the marrow does not adequately replace red blood cells. Acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب) and its cold‑agglutinin subtype (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد) are both autoimmune hemolytic processes in which antibodies target red blood cells, leading to their premature destruction. In addition, inflammatory arthritis (التهاب المفاصل) is included as an autoimmune joint disease characterized by chronic inflammation and tissue damage. Together, these entities illustrate how autoimmune mechanisms can affect both blood formation and joint health, underscoring the importance of recognizing their shared immunologic basis for accurate diagnosis and coordinated patient management.
